coredata.dc:description: Nanofluids-based direct absorption solar collectors are promising candidates for medium–high-temperature solar energy harvesting. However, nanofluids’ complicated preparation process and undesirable high-temperature stability have hindered their practical applications. Herein, we propose a facile method for synthesizing gold/carbon quantum dots (Au-CQDs) nanofluids by directly carbonizing the base fluid and spontaneously assembling with Au nanoparticles (AuNPs) triggered by high temperatures. The results indicate that the self-assembled Au-CQDs nanofluids can maintain high stability at 110 °C for 100 h without precipitation and keep excellent photothermal conversion performance under 10 sun irradiation. The concentration and particle size of AuNPs are crucial factors affecting the self-assembly process. By modulating the microscopic morphologies of the self-assembled nanoparticles, the extinction coefficient of the prepared nanofluids is up to 88.7 % at a low loading of 30 ppm. The nanofluids can reach an equilibrium temperature of 50 °C under 1 sun irradiation, 10.4 °C higher than the base fluid due to the enhanced plasmonic effects and stability resulting from the CQDs dotted AuNPs. This work offers a new strategy to fabricate highly stable nanofluids with excellent light absorption properties for efficient solar thermal applications.

head.ce:title: Self-assembled Au-CQDs nanofluids with excellent solar absorption and medium–high temperature stability for solar energy harvesting

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nanofluids-based direct absorption solar collectors are promising candidates for medium–high-temperature solar energy harvesting. However, nanofluids’ complicated preparation process and undesirable high-temperature stability have hindered their practical applications. Herein, we propose a facile method for synthesizing gold/carbon quantum dots (Au-CQDs) nanofluids by directly carbonizing the base fluid and spontaneously assembling with Au nanoparticles (AuNPs) triggered by high temperatures. The results indicate that the self-assembled Au-CQDs nanofluids can maintain high stability at 110 °C for 100 h without precipitation and keep excellent photothermal conversion performance under 10 sun irradiation. The concentration and particle size of AuNPs are crucial factors affecting the self-assembly process. By modulating the microscopic morphologies of the self-assembled nanoparticles, the extinction coefficient of the prepared nanofluids is up to 88.7 % at a low loading of 30 ppm. The nanofluids can reach an equilibrium temperature of 50 °C under 1 sun irradiation, 10.4 °C higher than the base fluid due to the enhanced plasmonic effects and stability resulting from the CQDs dotted AuNPs. This work offers a new strategy to fabricate highly stable nanofluids with excellent light absorption properties for efficient solar thermal applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Solar energy, the most abundant renewable energy source on the planet, has the advantage of being both green and clean [1]. Among various solar thermal conversion techniques [2,3], nanofluids-based direct absorption solar collectors (DASCs) are promising candidates for medium–high-temperature solar energy harvesting [4]. Although significant progress has been achieved in this field, realizing practical applications of DASCs under medium–high temperature conditions with concentrated solar irradiation remains challenging [5].

Nanofluids with single nanoparticles typically have a limited light absorption range [6,7]. For this reason, researchers have employed composite materials to broaden the light absorption range based on their fundamental mechanisms of photothermal conversion. Plasmonic nanoparticles have far superior extinction performance in the visible light range than other materials (semiconductors and carbon materials) due to the localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) effect [8], making them commonly used in synthesizing composite nanofluids. For example, Xuan et al. [9] blended magnetic Fe3O4 with TiN nanoparticles to make water-based nanofluids capable of absorbing the full solar spectra. He et al. [10] synthesized a composite nanoparticle with hedgehog morphology by combining ZnO with Au nanoparticles (AuNPs), and the nanofluids could reach an equilibrium temperature of 125 °C within 1 h at 10 kW/m2. In addition, one of the practical ideas to enhance solar absorption is the fabrication of core–shell structures with various light absorption materials [11,12]. For example, Wen et al. [13] prepared TiN@SiCw binary composite nanoparticles, which realized 96.2 % of the absorption fraction and 90.5 % of the photothermal efficiency at the highest concentration of 140 ppm. Shang et al. [14] synthesized Ag@Al2O3 nanofluids with excellent light absorption and heat conversion performance, which could reach 90.5 °C in an irradiation test at a concentration of 0.04 wt%.

Although composite nanofluids can significantly improve the absorption capability of the base fluid, their effectiveness is primarily restricted to low-temperature applications [15,16]. Moreover, especially under high-temperature and high-flux irradiation conditions, the high enthalpy of nanoparticles tends to lead to a high surface energy, causing transient instability [17]. Hence, many researchers have adopted surface modification strategies to obtain high-temperature stable nanofluids [4]. In this point, the core–shell structure (with silica as the outer shell) can reduce the tendency of nanoparticles to agglomerate due to Gibbs free energy changes within the nanofluid. Han et al. [18] discovered that the Ag@SiO2/PG nanofluids exhibited good thermal stability at 95 °C and 120 °C. Lee et al. [19] synthesized Au@SiO2 nanofluids that remained stably dispersed after exposure to thermal testing at 150 °C for one hour. In addition, Deng et al. [20] modified the graphene nanosheets by oxidative etching, and the graphene nanofluids could maintain uniform dispersion at 200 °C for 50 h. Despite the advancements that improve the high-temperature stability of nanofluids, the complicated and time-consuming preparation process still poses challenges for the large-scale production and commercialization of nanofluids [21,22].

On the other hand, besides a series of traditional or chemically modified nanofluids, a class of environmentally friendly carbon quantum dots (CQDs) is also gaining popularity among researchers due to their excellent thermal stability and simple preparation process. Such as Seifikar et al. [23] prepared CQDs/PEG nanofluids using microwave heating of PEG 200, which showed long-term stability at 200 °C and reached 60 °C after irradiation with 0.8 sun for 100 min. They noted that the CQDs nanofluids have many advantages, including simply preparing, low cost, without dispersant, excellent high-thermal stability, and high photothermal efficiency, which indicates great potential for medium–high temperature solar energy harvesting. In addition, it has been shown that it is possible to synthesize nanofluids with solar selectivity using waste engine oils and apply them to high temperatures [24]. This is in line with the idea of synthesizing CQDs nanofluids. Note that the light absorption range of CQDs is generally narrow, which is mainly exhibited in the ultraviolet region (200 nm–400 nm). Thus, the development of CQDs in photothermal utilization is constrained due to the wastage of solar radiation in other bands. However, the intense fluorescence effect makes it more applicable in photovoltaics and catalysis [25].

Recently, the self-assembly technology has been applied to prepare composite nanofluids to achieve high-temperature stability and light absorption enhancement [26]. The nanoparticles obtained by self-assembly usually have long-term stability due to low Gibbs free energy [27,28]. In addition, many researchers have suggested that elevated temperatures might trigger the self-organization of nanomaterials, resulting in the formation of unique nanostructures. This phenomenon has been effectively applied to augment boiling heat transfer [29]. Therefore, material compounding and high-temperature stabilization are expected simultaneously through self-assembly [30]. As presented in Table 1 , the microstructures of nanomaterials prepared by self-assembly could be precisely controlled, and their structural stability is guaranteed. The evidence above suggests that microscopic design plays a vital role in macroscopic property modulation of nanoparticles [31]. Nevertheless, current research on self-assembly focuses on synthesizing functional materials, which is time-consuming and complicated. More importantly, simultaneously enhancing nanofluids' solar absorption and thermal stability remains a significant challenge, and the constitutive mechanism between structure and performance needs to be clarified [32].

The above studies show that metallic-based materials usually have a high potential to meet the heat transfer requirements of medium–high temperature DASCs [42]. Moreover, they have high extinction coefficients in the visible region and were often modified to prepare composite nanofluids aiming to broaden the absorption range [43]. However, their long-term stability in high temperatures is still a challenge. Some researchers believe that the excellent thermal stability and versatile morphology of green carbon-based materials have advantages in material composite and interface assembly of nanofluids, which can broaden the solar absorption bands of other photothermal materials and achieve a qualitative improvement in the photothermal conversion efficiency [44,45]. The self-assembly technique can effectively integrate multi-materials into an ordered structure, giving it high stability and ensuring that its optical and thermal properties are simultaneously enhanced.

Herein, we propose a cost-effective self-assembly strategy for synthesizing metal–carbon composite nanofluids. The AuNPs with oil solubility were uniformly dispersed in therminol®66 (T66), and CQDs were generated and then inlaid on the outer surface of AuNPs during the high-temperature carbonization of T66. It is found that the high-temperature environment induces self-assembly and enhances nanofluids' high-temperature stability. The basic structural units of Au-CQDs are arranged in a fixed order, forming a tessellation structure with a core–shell-like morphology. This unique structure allows the composite material to have considerably superior light absorption capability over the simple mixing one while maintaining good dispersion in T66 at ambient temperature for 30 days or at 110 °C for 100 h and keeping excellent photothermal conversion performance in high-flux irradiation. In addition, the self-assembled Au-CQDs nanofluids exhibit a solar-weighted absorption coefficient of 88.7 % at 30 ppm and obtain an equilibrium temperature of 50 °C under 1 sun irradiation. Our findings confirm the advantages of self-assembly technology for nanofluid preparation and offer an innovative strategy for fabricating high-performance solar nanofluids.

Tetrachloroauric acid tetrahydrate (HAuCl4·4H2O, ≥99 %, trace metals basis) was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., China. Oleylamine (C18H37N, AR, 90 %), Cyclohexane (C6H12, AR, 99.5 %), and Sodium hydroxide (NaOH, ACS, 97 %) were received from Aladdin. Therminol®66 (T66) was purchased from Suzhou Shouno Heat Transfer Oil Co., Ltd., China. Deionized water (Shanghai-Hetai, ECO-Q15) was used for all experiments. All chemicals were used without purification.

AuNPs were prepared using a modified Turkevich method [26], replacing sodium citrate with oleylamine as the reducing and modifying agent [46]. Chloroauric acid (1 wt%) was first added to deionized water at a specific ratio and heated to a boiling state with magnetic stirring at 800 rpm. Then, a certain amount of oleylamine was added to the solution, kept boiling for 90 min, and cooled to the ambient temperature. During this process, the color of the solution gradually changed from orange-yellow to light-yellow, and then from pink to deep burgundy. Next, 30 ml of cyclohexane was added, and the mixture was thoroughly stirred at 1000 rpm for 60 min. Finally, the mixture's pH was adjusted by adding some NaOH solution, and the stirring continued for another 60 min. After stewing overnight, the solution appeared to be stratified. The upper transparent layer (Au colloids) was collected and stored. Then, the Au colloids were mixed with T66 at ambient temperature and dispersed through a 150 W ultrasonicator for 5 min to obtain the Au/T66 nanofluids (Au nanofluids).

As shown in Fig. S2a, T66, the carbon source was first loaded into a container and sealed. Then, the container was heated at 150 °C for 20 h using a heating plate and subsequently cooled to the ambient temperature. The color of the NF sample changed from initially transparent to light yellow, indicating that CQDs nanofluids were synthesized [47].

Like the preparation process of CQDs nanofluids, the freshly prepared Au/T66 nanofluids (10 ppm, 20 ppm, 30 ppm, 40 ppm, 50 ppm) were placed on a heating plate first (no magnetic stirring), and the temperature was set to 150 °C for 20 h. The reaction vessel is a cylindrical transparent glass bottle with a bottom area of 4.9 cm2, a height of 5.5 cm, and a sealed cap on its top (Fig. S1a). After heating, the samples were cooled to the ambient temperature. The color of the solution changed from burgundy to dark red or dark brown, indicating that Au-CQDs composite nanofluids were synthesized.

Special Aberration Corrected Transmission Electron Microscope (AC-TEM, Titan G2 60–300) was used to check the surface morphology, size, and shapes of AuNPs, CQDs, and Au-CQDs NPs. The morphology of AuNPs before self-assembly was investigated by bright-field and selective area electron diffraction (SAED), which was performed in TEM mode operated at 300 kV. The energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy mapping (EDX mapping) of the Au-CQDs NPs was added to identify the elemental distribution based on the bright-field image. In addition, the particle size distributions in nanofluids were monitored by dynamic light scattering (DLS, Brookhaven, 90plus). The fluorescence spectra of CQDs nanofluids at different excitation wavelengths were recorded by a spectrofluorometer (Shimadzu, RF-6000) equipped with a Xenon lamp. Moreover, a thermogravimetric infrared gas chromatograph (TGA-GCMS-FTIR, PerkinElmer TGA 8000) analyzed CQDs NF samples' thermogravimetric curves, infrared spectra, and chemical components. The specific heat capacity of NF samples was determined by differential scanning calorimetry (DSC, NETZSCH, 214F). The absorption spectra of NF samples were measured using ultraviolet–visible-near infrared spectroscopy (UV–Vis-NIR, Shimadzu, UV-3600i plus) from 350 nm to 1100 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physicochemical properties of AuNPs and CQD nanofluids
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the morphology and crystallinity of AuNPs, we employed TEM bright-field and SAED analysis. The AuNPs present natural dispersion because the morphological distortion between Au crystals is not easily caused by recrystallization after modification by oleylamine (Fig. 1 a–b). Also, the AuNPs exhibit a size ranging from 5 nm to 15 nm, which agrees well with the 522 nm absorption peaks resulting from the localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) effects of Au nanofluids observed by other researchers [8] (Fig. 1e). Via the fast Fourier transform (FFT), several diffraction rings were observed in Fig. 1c, especially the typical cubic crystal diffraction rings such as (331), (311), (220), (200), and (111), indicating that the synthesized AuNPs mainly consist of Au cubic crystals agglomerated with a single crystalline phase without doping. A high-resolution image (Fig. 1d) shows the lattice stripes of Au in Fig. 1b with (111) reflection, which implies that the differences between the particles synthesized under the same preparation process are negligible.

Previous research shows that chemical coating on the outer surface of nanoparticles (mainly composed of long-chain polymorphs) based on a one-step process can enhance the stability of nanofluids [48]. However, the nanoparticles still tend to aggregate due to the strong Brownian motion [49]. The nanofluid samples (NF samples) were stored in a dark ambient environment for five months of aging tests to evaluate the long-term stability of the oleylamine-modified Au nanofluids [50]. Fig. 1e compares UV–Vis-NIR absorption spectra of the fresh and aged NF samples. The plasmonic peak of the NF samples decreased by 1.45 % after long-term storage. When Au atoms were polymerized into Au particles of a specific size, the oleylamine's long chains were wrapped in layers and constituted a stable solid layer, which enhances the compatibility of the Au nuclei with the oil-based liquid, ensuring them less prone to chemical deconstruction, macroscopic dissolution, and deposition under Brownian collisions [51]. Meanwhile, to confirm the stability of Au nanofluids in the aging experiment, we conducted dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurements to examine the particle size variation. The average particle size only changed by 3.33 % between the aged and fresh NF samples (Fig. 1f), suggesting the Au nanofluid's high stability. In summary, the long-term chemical stability of AuNPs allows them to maintain a stable dispersion in T66, laying the foundation for the self-assembly of the two materials later.

Similar to the mechanism of microwave heating the PEG to synthesize CQDs nanofluids [52], high-temperature causes a gradual carbonization reaction in T66, which is a synthetic heat transfer oil with modified terphenyl (characterized by long-chain alkane branched chains) as the main component. In a high-temperature confined space, the benzene rings and long-chain alkane molecules of modified triphenylene are randomly broken in oxygen and slowly decomposed by oxidation into short-chain alkane molecular fragments. During the heating process, the hydrophobic ends of the short-chain alkene fragments are continuously aggregated, and the external passivation layer is densely encapsulated, eventually forming CQDs [53]. An apparent Tindal effect appeared in the fluid under laser irradiation (Fig. S1), indicating that the CQDs are uniformly dispersed in T66. The light-to-dark color of the NF samples shows that the concentration of CQDs in T66 is gradually increased.

Existing studies have shown a photoluminescence phenomenon of CQDs nanofluids, and the wavelength of their emitted light is a red shift with the increasing particle size [54]. To identify that the modified hydrothermal method could obtain CQDs from T66 in a high-temperature environment, we measured the fluorescence excitation spectra of the fabricated nanofluids and normalized them with a spectrofluorometer. The results agree well with the fluorescence spectra of CQDs nanofluids prepared by microwave heating PEG method [23]. The fluorescence spectra in different intensities illustrate the presence of CQDs of varying size (Fig. 1g). These particles possess the ability to absorb photons in different wavelengths, converting the energy of photons from solar radiation into a mixture of light and heat, then radiating them out, which is the fundamental reason that CQDs nanofluids can be captured by thermal infrared imaging and fluorescence photometry [55]. Morphological images of the particles in the NF samples were taken using TEM (Fig. 1h). The synthesized CQDs were irregularly polygonal in shape (particle size between 1–10 nm) and uniformly dispersed. In addition, we further analyzed the thermal decomposition and chemical composition of CQDs nanofluids. A primary decomposition temperature was observed at 185 °C due to the flash point of T66 (∼184 °C), and the nanoparticles decomposed at 329 °C, leading to a secondary decomposition point (Fig. 1i). The GCMS analysis showed that the decomposition gas at this state consisted of three main products: H2O, C6H16, and CO2, which also indicates that the constituent elements of CQDs are mainly C and H. This is consistent with the characteristics of the NF samples prepared by hydrothermal and microwave methods in the literature [56]. However, aromatic hydrocarbons are usually poorly resistant to high temperatures and can easily cause multilevel cracking, so strict control of temperature and reaction environment is required [57]. In addition, an aging test was carried out for five months to confirm the long-term stability of CQDs nanofluids even when subjected to high temperatures. See Note 1 in the Supporting Materials for more details.

As a potent reducing agent, oleylamine has good water–oil amphiphilicity due to its long carbon chain structure [46]. Oleylamine reduced the Au ions in chloroauric acid to Au atoms, forming AuNPs through metal bonds subsequently, and grafted the amino end on the outer surface of AuNPs (Fig. 2 a). The carbon chain grafted on the outer layer of AuNPs stretched in T66, preventing AuNPs from agglomeration via spatial resistance [58]. During the heating process, the modified triplets in T66 gradually broke, and the hydrophobic ends of the short-chain olefin fragments agglomerated to nucleate, forming CQDs with negatively charged carboxyl groups distributed at their free ends [59]. Due to the positive charge on the outer layer of AuNPs and the negative charge on the outer layer of CQDs, both of them were electrostatically attracted by each other (Fig. 2b). Besides, free CQDs gradually converged towards the AuNPs under the effect of gravitational force because of the low concentration of AuNPs (ppm) and excess CQDs in T66, leading the positively charged AuNPs to be surrounded by the negatively charged CQDs. According to the theory of Yang et al. [60], a condensation reaction between the carboxyl group and the amino group occurs, forming a polymeric bond that provides the conditions for the self-assembly of AuNPs and CQDs. Furthermore, the self-assembly process above was continuously advanced with the generation of CQDs at high temperatures.

To clarify the microscopic morphology of the Au-CQDs nanoparticles (Au-CQDs NPs), we examined the NF samples after self-assembly via TEM and EDX mapping. The Au-CQDs NPs still exhibited a spherical structure after self-assembly (Fig. 2c–d), indicating that CQDs would not form large clusters with polygonal fragments but are closely adsorbed on the outer layer of the Au nucleus in the form of tiny microparticles, forming a loose and porous encapsulated shell without dense coherence.

Based on the fluorescence properties of different materials, the emission spectra of the nanofluids before and after self-assembly were measured at an excitation wavelength of 365 nm (where CQDs have the most substantial fluorescence emission effect under UV lamp [23]) by a spectrofluorometer. Before self-assembly, the nanofluids without CQDs only exhibited an Au characteristic peak at 400 nm under the irradiation of a UV lamp (Fig. 2e), indicating that the Au nanofluids' fluorescent color is purple as observed. The CQD shell's blocking action would render the Au characteristic's peak invisible. After forming a CQDs shell, the fluorescence peak of CQDs nanofluid appeared near 450 nm, and the color presented pure white, contributing to the impurities of olefin polymers. Compared with AuNPs, CQDs adsorbed on the outside were tiny in size, which enables the fluorescence intensity of the synthesized Au-CQDs nanofluids to be significantly lower than Au nanofluids. Furthermore, high temperatures intensify nanoparticle collisions, leading to agglomerative deposition [61]. In addition, the generation of CQDs and the agglomeration of AuNPs co-occurred with a specific stochastic nature during self-assembly, which may cause the failure of self-assembly. See Note 2 in the Supporting Materials for more details about the failure modes of the self-assembly process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Modulation of the light absorption properties of Au-CQDs nanofluids
ce:section.ce:para: According to Section 2.2, the concentration of AuNPs (which determines the light absorption property) and the particle size of AuNPs (which determines the high-temperature stability) are two crucial factors affecting the self-assembly process of Au-CQDs nanofluids. To explore the conformational relationship between the Au-CQDs NPs' microscopic morphology and macroscopic optical properties, we further conducted Au-CQDs assembly experiments with different Au particle sizes (5 nm and 15 nm) from 10 ppm to 50 ppm. A UV–Vis-NIR spectrophotometer measured the absorption spectra every four hours to monitor the assembly progress. Note that the self-assembly reaction would fail when no AuNPs were added, and only pure CQDs nanofluids were synthesized at that time, which shows weak light absorption capabilities. (See Note 3 in the Supporting Materials for more details). When AuNPs were dispersed in T66, the essential condition was in place, and the assembly reaction would start immediately at high temperatures. The changes in absorption spectra of Au-CQDs nanofluids are shown in Fig. 3 a–f. An increase was observed during heating, suggesting that the synthesis of CQDs triggered the self-assembly reaction. For 10 ppm Au nanofluids, the absorption curve decreased in 4 h–8 h (Fig. 3a), which implies that the incomplete formation of CQDs and the low attraction results in the agglomeration and dissolution of AuNPs. As self-assembly proceeds, CQDs were gradually produced and coated on the outer layer of AuNPs, increasing the absorption. Note that the agglomeration of AuNPs is irreversible, so the absorption improvement is mainly because of the synthesis of CQDs and a small amount of Au-CQDs, implying the failure of self-assembly. From 30 ppm to 50 ppm, the intense attraction between AuNPs and CQDs could block the formation of large-sized CQDs and attract CQDs to the vicinity of the Au nucleus in small nanoparticles (Fig. 3b–c). The passivation layer of a robust shell of CQDs reduced the Brownian motion-induced particle dissipation, forming Au-CQDs nanofluids and gradually increasing absorption [62]. Fig. 3g shows the average size of Au-CQDs nanofluids after self-assembly. The size of Au-CQDs nanofluids formed by 10 ppm Au nanofluids was the largest due to the severe agglomerative deposition in this system. With the increase of Au loading, the average size increased accordingly, implying the generation of Au-CQDs. The shell of Au-CQDs NPs was thick and loose at 50 ppm, contributing to ample free CQDs in the fluid with high disorder and agglomeration tendency, giving rise to a significant deviation of the measured values.

Fig. 3d–f depicts the absorption curves of nanofluids by adjusting the au size to 15 nm without changing other conditions. The increase in the number of groups brought about by the large surface area of AuNPs attracted more CQDs around, accelerating the self-assembly speed of Au-CQDs, thus avoiding the failure of self-assembly at 10 ppm (no decrease in absorption curve and offset of the absorption peak [34]). The absorption curve steadily rose when AuNPs reached 30 ppm (Fig. 3e), suggesting that the Au-CQDs nanofluids are stable. Notably, the accelerated assembly rate is accompanied by an increase in the thickness of the formed CQDs shell. The absorption peak of Au-CQDs nanofluid disappeared at 50 ppm (Fig. 3f), which indicates that the over-thick shell obscured the LSPR effect of AuNPs, making the Au-CQDs NPs prone to agglomeration. The process above was also proved from the perspective of particle size variations (Fig. 3g–h).

Furthermore, we calculated the solar-weighted absorption fraction to evaluate the light absorption properties of single and composite nanofluids (See Note 4 in the Supporting Materials for more details). From Au nanofluids to Au-CQDs nanofluids (only 10 ppm), the extinction coefficient increased from 7.5 % to 31.9 % (at 15 nm) and 6.9 % to 24.7 % (at 5 nm) respectively, which indicates that the unique microstructure of Au-CQDs NPs has a cooperative promotion on the enhancement of the light absorption property of nanofluids, far exceeding the simple mixture of AuNPs and CQDs NPs. This phenomenon is mainly due to the higher LSPR effect of AuNPs by the externally embedded CQDs NPs, so the Au-CQDs NPs obtain an elevated absorption spectrum [63]. As shown in Fig. 3i, the solar-weighted absorption fraction was enhanced with the rising size of AuNPs. This is because AuNPs with 15 nm Au core could rapidly form a high-temperature resistance on the outer layer at the early stage, avoiding the Van der Waals clustering of AuNPs, which is definitive to smooth self-assembly later. Based on the premise of stable dispersion, the solar-weighted absorption fraction of Au-CQDs nanofluids increased from 22.9 % to 88.7 % (15 nm) and 22.3 % to 51.7 % (5 nm) in 30 ppm, indicating that larger-sized AuNPs ensure the Au-CQDs nanofluids to obtain better photothermal conversion performance than Au nanofluids.

In addition, the enhancement in light absorption is more pronounced in the presence of larger-sized AuNPs because CQDs are more densely and coherently encapsulated in the outer shell, simultaneously improving the high-temperature stability and light absorption property of the Au-CQDs. Besides, an improvement of below 5 % was found from 30 ppm to 50 ppm (15 nm), suggesting that the enhancement is offset by the instability within the Au-CQDs nanofluids, reducing the positive impact of self-assembly. On the contrary, a similar phenomenon does not occur when the AuNPs are 5 nm due to the agglomeration of Au-CQDs NPs in the later stage being weaker at this time. Moreover, the absorption performance of Au-CQDs nanofluids in 50 ppm (5 nm) is lower than 40 ppm (15 nm), and increasing the concentration to improve solar absorption would bring high synthesis costs, which is the reason why AuNPs with small particle sizes (5 nm) are eliminated.

In conclusion, the average extinction coefficient of Au-CQDs nanofluids reached up to 88.7 % for the concentration around 30 ppm, and the nanofluids exhibited high-temperature stability and high absorption simultaneously during the self-assembly process. Note that the composite materials showed relatively low extinction properties in the near-infrared region (Fig. S5), implying that more technologies should further enhance nanofluids' solar absorption performance.

The stability of composite nanofluids is vital for practical medium–high temperature DASC applications. The self-assembly process was achieved by a continuous heating operation at 150 °C for 20 h, corresponding to a thermal stability test of the Au-CQDs nanofluids for 20 h. We evaluated the degree of agglomeration inside the nanofluids by analyzing the particle size variations (Fig. 3g–h). At 10 ppm, the sharp decrease suggests the failure of self-assembly. At 30 ppm, compared with Au nanofluids, the particle size of Au-CQDs NPs changed slightly, implying that the agglomeration inside the fluid is weak. At 50 ppm, the Au-CQDs NPs exhibited shell adhesion due to Brownian motion, resulting in a larger particle size (See Note 5 in the Supporting Materials for more details). However, the average particle size inside the fluid increased five times after the self-assembly process, regardless of the particle sizes of AuNPs. Due to the measurement error of DLS in the oil phase and the effect of CQDs' protective layers on scattering [64], the agglomeration was considered extremely weak, and the Au-CQDs nanofluids were well dispersed. This can be attributed to the defense effect of the homogeneous CQD passivation layers on the surface of AuNPs, which introduces high thermal resistance between AuNPs and T66 and blocks, to some extent, the deconstruction and thermal collisions of AuNPs at high temperatures [65].

To further investigate Au-CQD nanofluids' stability, we carried out aging and continuous heating tests on 30 ppm and 50 ppm NF samples. The absorbance of Au-CQDs nanofluids decreased by 9.5 % at the absorption peak, and the average particle size increased by 9.4 nm after 30 days of storage in the dark and the ambient temperature (Fig. 4 a–b), indicating that Au-CQDs nanofluids can be stably dispersed for a long time. Subsequently, the NF samples were placed on a heating plate and continuously heated at 110 °C for 100 h. The absorption peak of Au-CQDs nanofluids decreased by 2.8 %, and the particle size increased by only 3.2 nm, which indicates negligible agglomeration inside the fluid (Fig. 4c–d). We noticed that the nanofluid stability at high temperatures is superior to that in ambient storage, mainly because continuous heating promotes the self-healing and adhesion of the CQD passivation layers, leading to composite nanoparticles still having low surface energy and maintaining stability.

As described in Section 2.3, the failure of self-assembly is caused by the irreversible agglomeration of AuNPs or the thick shell of CQDs. To avoid that, more sophisticated sealing means or intervention of other surfactants/protective layers are required to enhance the contact thermal resistance of AuNPs and cut off the further proliferation of the CQDs shells, which is an effective strategy to improve the long-term stability of Au-CQDs nanofluids at higher temperatures.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The photothermal conversion performance of Au-CQD nanofluids
ce:section.ce:para: The photothermal conversion performances of the as-prepared nanofluids were evaluated by the simulated volumetric solar conversion system (Fig. 4e), consisting of a solar simulator (CEL-S500R3, Ceaulight), a thermocouple (OMEGA, 5TC-TT-T-30-SLE), and a data acquisition system. The thermocouple was inserted vertically into the middle of the rectangular cuvette container (1 cm long, 1 cm wide, and 4.2 cm high) in the center of the insulated outer layer at a distance of approximately 2 cm from the bottom and a distance of approximately 0.5 cm from the four walls of the cuvette. Nanofluids were irradiated under a solar simulator at 100 mW/cm2 (1 sun) for 45 min and then naturally cooled for 15 min.

Under solar irradiation, the temperatures of nanofluids gradually increased with time and eventually reached the equilibrium. According to Fig. 4f, the maximum equilibrium temperature was 47.5 °C for Au nanofluids at 50 ppm, and the temperature rise was 22.6 °C. Although the absorption of pure CQDs nanofluids is not strong (due to the low concentration of CQDs), their equilibrium temperature is even higher than Au nanofluids at 10 ppm. There are two reasons for this phenomenon. On the one hand, AuNPs have a strong LSPR effect that leads to a more intense scattering effect, increasing the optical losses and thus reducing the photothermal conversion efficiency [66]. On the other hand, CQDs with different particle sizes can absorb photons located in specific wavelengths due to their different band gaps. The energy difference caused by the atomic energy leap is transformed into photons with different energies and radiated through the photoluminescence effect. Subsequently, these photons are again absorbed by CQDs of other sizes. This mode enables CQDs to utilize solar energy step by step, exhibiting superior photothermal conversion performance at the macroscopic level [23].

The photothermal conversion performance of Au-CQDs nanofluids was substantially improved compared to Au nanofluids (Fig. 4g). The equilibrium temperature reached 44.3 °C, 49.4 °C, and 50.2 °C for the concentration of 10 ppm, 30 ppm, and 50 ppm, respectively, and the corresponding temperature rise value was 19.3 °C, 24.6 °C and 25.1 °C. As presented in Fig. 4h, the photothermal conversion performance of the nanofluids was in the following order: CQDs/T66 > Au/T66 > Au-CQDs/T66. In detail, the equilibrium temperature of Au-CQDs nanofluids was 3.6 °C and 10.7 °C higher than that of Au nanofluids and base fluid, respectively. This result indicates that the tessellated lattice structures reduce the radiant heat loss and capture more solar radiation, enhancing system efficiency. Besides, the temperature variation of Au-CQDs nanofluids is higher than that of Au nanofluids for concentrations higher than 10 ppm (Fig. 4i), suggesting a higher photothermal conversion efficiency due to the excellent temperature stability and light absorption capability of the self-assembled nanofluids.

ce:section.ce:section-title: High-flux irradiation stability of Au-CQDs nanofluids
ce:section.ce:para: Apart from the instability due to high temperatures, exposure to solar radiation would induce morphological changes in the composite nanoparticles, such as adsorbed layer detachment, leading to nanofluid fragmentation and dissolution. Moreover, although ultraviolet (UV) light accounts for only a tiny fraction of solar radiation, its high energy, especially in the case of high-flux radiation, will cause severe erosion of the functional groups on the surface of the nanoparticles, resulting in the abortion of their photothermal conversion [67].

Therefore, to verify the stability of Au-CQDs nanofluids under high-flux irradiation to adapt to the working environment of medium–high temperature DASCs, high-flux irradiation experiment was carried out on 10 ppm, 30 ppm, and 50 ppm Au-CQDs nanofluids at 1000 mW/cm2 (10 sun) irradiation intensity using the simulated volumetric solar conversion system in Section 3.5. The absorption spectra, particle size distribution, temperature evolution curves, and temperature variations were compared before and after the irradiation, as shown in Fig. 5 .

After 10 sun irradiation, the changes in the absorption spectra of Au-CQDs nanofluids at different concentrations rarely shift, indicating their excellent stability under high-flux irradiation (Fig. 5a–c). The absorption spectra of all samples show a slight decrease before 400 nm, which may be attributed to the corrosion of the passivation layer of CQDs of Au-CQDs NPs by the UV light with higher energy, leading to the surface oxidization of the ligand at the passivation end, finally affecting the light absorption property of CQDs in the UV region. This corrosive effect is most pronounced at 10 ppm (with the largest shift in the absorption spectra), probably due to the higher concentration of large CQDs NPs inside the fluid at this time (Note 2), which makes the oxidation of the passivation layer of the CQDs more obviously. This analysis can be re-confirmed by the changes in the particle size distribution: at concentrations of 10 ppm, 30 ppm, and 50 ppm, the sizes of Au-CQDs increased by 12.5 nm, 0.3 nm, and 52.7 nm after irradiation, respectively (Fig. 5d–f). In particular, at 50 ppm, high-flux irradiation accelerates the agglomeration rate of the Au-CQDs NPs inside the fluid, leading to a significant change between the two results. In addition, the best stability of Au-CQDs nanofluids under high-flux irradiation is observed at a concentration of 30 ppm, similar to the conclusions in Section 3.4. In conclusion, the high-flux irradiation stability of the Au-CQDs nanofluids at variation concentration is in the following order: 30 ppm > 50 ppm > 10 ppm.

As shown in Fig. 5g, the higher the concentration of Au-CQDs NPs, the higher the equilibrium temperature in the photothermal conversion tests. The equilibrium temperatures of Au-CQDs nanofluids at 10 ppm, 30 ppm, and 50 ppm were 146.1 °C, 163.9 °C, and 165.1 °C, respectively. Note that the Au-CQDs nanofluids still maintain excellent photothermal performance and exhibit a smoother temperature curve, especially at 30 ppm (Fig. 5h). In addition, the equilibrium temperature of Au-CQDs nanofluid at 30 ppm were 39.6 °C and 27.5 °C higher than T66 and CQDs nanofluid, respectively, proving that the Au-CQDs nanofluid still presents excellent photothermal conversion performance under the intense irradiation corrosion of 10 sun (Fig. 5i). Furthermore, compared to Au nanofluids (which are unstable under 10 sun radiation), the Au-CQDs nanofluids were stable during photothermal conversion process. This is due to the contact thermal resistances formed by the solid CQDs in the outer layer of the AuNPs, making it possible to continue the photothermal conversion under higher solar radiation (more than 10 sun).

In this work, we proposed a self-assembly strategy to fabricate Au-CQDs nanofluids with high-temperature stability and excellent light absorption performance. By modulating the microscopic morphologies of the self-assembled nanoparticles, the solar-weighted absorption fraction of Au-CQDs nanofluids could be increased from 22.9 % to 88.7 % at 30 ppm without adding other materials compared to Au nanofluids, and the maximum temperature of Au-CQDs nanofluids of 30 ppm was 10.4 °C higher than base fluid under 1 sun irradiation. Besides, the self-assembled Au-CQDs nanofluids could have stable dispersion at 110 °C for 100 h and keep excellent photothermal conversion under 10 sun irradiation. This investigation demonstrates the superiority of the self-assembled nanofluids and provides a reference for preparing nanofluids for medium–high temperature DASC applications. However, it should be noted that the long-term stability of Au-CQDs nanofluids at higher operating temperatures is still challenging, which deserves more in-depth investigations, and further optimization of the self-assembly process is also needed to realize full-spectrum solar thermal harvesting.
